events.title: Computing Machinery and Intelligence
events.short: Poses “can machines think?” and reframes it as the Imitation Game.
events.long: Turing sidesteps defining 'thinking' by proposing an imitation game — now the Turing Test — in which a machine passes if its text responses are indistinguishable from a human's. The paper systematically anticipates and rebuts the major objections to machine intelligence, from mathematical to theological. It also sketches the idea of a learning machine that could be educated like a child rather than programmed outright. Its framing shaped how the field would think about and measure machine intelligence for decades.
events.impact: Established the foundational question and an operational test for machine intelligence.

events.title: Dartmouth Summer Research Project on Artificial Intelligence
events.short: Coins the term “artificial intelligence” and launches the field.
events.long: The proposal convened researchers for a summer workshop premised on the conjecture that learning and every other feature of intelligence can in principle be described precisely enough for a machine to simulate them. It coined the name 'artificial intelligence' and laid out an agenda spanning language, neural nets, abstraction, and self-improvement. The gathering brought together many of the people who would define the field's first decades. It is widely regarded as the founding event of AI as an organized discipline.
events.impact: Founded AI as a named research discipline.

events.title: The Perceptron: A Probabilistic Model for Information Storage
events.short: Introduces a trainable single-layer neural classifier that learns from data.
events.long: Rosenblatt's perceptron is a weighted threshold unit whose weights are adjusted by a simple error-correction rule so it can learn to separate linearly separable patterns. It was realized both as an algorithm and as physical hardware, and it learned a classification task directly from examples rather than explicit programming. The work drew enormous early attention to the idea of brain-inspired learning machines. It pioneered the connectionist approach that, after later setbacks, would eventually flower into deep learning.
events.impact: Pioneered learnable artificial neurons and the connectionist approach.

events.title: Perceptrons
events.short: Proves single-layer perceptrons cannot represent functions like XOR.
events.long: Minsky and Papert formally analyze the perceptron's representational limits, proving that a single-layer network cannot compute non-linearly-separable functions such as XOR. Although multi-layer networks could in principle overcome this, no effective way to train them was then known. The critique dampened funding and enthusiasm for neural approaches and helped redirect AI toward symbolic methods. It is often cited as a trigger of the first AI winter for connectionist research.
events.impact: Exposed the limits of shallow networks and chilled neural-net research for years.

events.title: Learning Representations by Back-Propagating Errors
events.short: Popularizes backpropagation for training multi-layer neural networks.
events.long: The paper shows how to train multi-layer networks by propagating output errors backward through the layers to compute gradients via the chain rule. This let hidden units discover useful internal representations automatically, overcoming the single-layer limits earlier critics had identified. While the underlying technique had appeared in other forms before, this work made it widely understood and practical for neural networks. It became the foundational training algorithm behind essentially all modern deep learning.
events.impact: Made deep multi-layer networks trainable and revived neural-network research.

events.title: Backpropagation Applied to Handwritten Zip Code Recognition
events.authors: LeCun et al., AT&T Bell Labs, 1989
events.short: Trains a convolutional network end-to-end to read handwritten digits.
events.long: LeCun applies backpropagation to a network with local, weight-shared convolutional filters that exploit the 2D spatial structure of images. Trained end to end on handwritten zip-code digits, it learned features directly from pixels rather than relying on hand-engineered ones. The system was deployed to read real postal mail, an early large-scale practical success for neural networks. It established the LeNet lineage of convolutional networks that later dominated computer vision.
events.impact: Introduced practical convolutional networks for image recognition.

events.title: Long Short-Term Memory
events.short: Adds gated memory cells so recurrent nets can learn long-range dependencies.
events.long: LSTM introduces a memory cell guarded by input, output, and (later) forget gates that regulate how information is stored, used, and discarded over time. This gating lets error signals persist across many time steps, directly addressing the vanishing-gradient problem that crippled earlier recurrent networks. As a result, models could finally learn dependencies spanning long sequences of text or signals. LSTMs became the dominant sequence architecture for over a decade, powering translation, speech, and handwriting systems until Transformers arrived.
events.impact: Became the dominant sequence model for over a decade before Transformers.

events.title: ImageNet Classification with Deep Convolutional Networks
events.short: A deep GPU-trained CNN crushes the ImageNet benchmark, igniting deep learning.
events.long: AlexNet is a deep convolutional network trained on GPUs using ReLU activations, dropout, and data augmentation that won the 2012 ImageNet competition by a wide margin over hand-engineered approaches. Its decisive result convinced the field that deep learning scaled with data and compute. The work showed that GPUs made training large networks practical, reshaping the hardware of AI research. It is widely credited with launching the modern deep-learning era.
events.impact: Triggered the modern deep-learning era in computer vision.

events.title: Generative Adversarial Networks
events.short: Pits a generator against a discriminator to learn to synthesize data.
events.long: GANs train two networks in a minimax game: a generator tries to produce realistic samples while a discriminator learns to tell real data from generated fakes. The adversarial signal drives the generator toward the true data distribution without ever computing an explicit likelihood. The framework produced strikingly realistic synthetic images and spawned a large family of variants and applications. It opened a major line of generative modeling that influenced image synthesis, style transfer, and beyond.
events.impact: Opened a major line of generative modeling for images and beyond.

events.title: Adam: A Method for Stochastic Optimization
events.short: Combines momentum and per-parameter adaptive learning rates for training.
events.long: Adam maintains running estimates of the first and second moments of each parameter's gradients and uses them to scale per-parameter step sizes, with bias correction for the early steps. By combining momentum with adaptive learning rates, it converges quickly and is robust across a wide range of models and hyperparameter choices. Its ease of use meant practitioners could train new architectures with little tuning. It rapidly became the default optimizer for deep learning.
events.impact: Became the default optimizer for training deep networks.

events.title: Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition
events.short: Adds skip connections so very deep networks stay trainable.
events.long: ResNet reformulates layers to learn residual functions relative to their inputs, added back through identity shortcut connections. These skip paths ease gradient flow and let networks hundreds of layers deep train without the accuracy degradation that had plagued very deep models. The approach won the 2015 ImageNet competition and set new records across vision tasks. Residual connections became a near-universal building block, including inside Transformers.
events.impact: Made very deep networks practical and standard across vision and beyond.

events.title: Batch Normalization
events.short: Normalizes layer activations per mini-batch to speed and stabilize training.
events.long: Batch normalization standardizes each layer's inputs using mini-batch statistics, then rescales and shifts them with learned parameters. By stabilizing the distribution of activations during training, it permits much higher learning rates and faster, more reliable convergence. It also provides a mild regularizing effect that can reduce the need for other techniques. It became a standard component in deep architectures and inspired a family of normalization methods.
events.impact: Became a standard building block that stabilized deep-network training.

events.title: Efficient Estimation of Word Representations
events.short: Learns dense word embeddings that capture semantic and syntactic relations.
events.long: Word2vec trains shallow skip-gram and CBOW models to predict words from their surrounding context, yielding dense vectors where semantically similar words sit close together. The resulting embeddings famously encode analogies through simple vector arithmetic, capturing both meaning and syntax. Its efficient training made high-quality embeddings practical to compute on very large corpora. The vectors became a standard input representation across a wide range of NLP systems.
events.impact: Mainstreamed distributed word embeddings as a building block for NLP.

events.title: Neural Machine Translation by Jointly Learning to Align and Translate
events.short: Adds an attention mechanism that lets the decoder focus on source words.
events.long: Rather than compressing an entire sentence into one fixed-length vector, this model lets the decoder attend over all encoder states and weight the most relevant source words at each output step. The learned soft alignment removed a key bottleneck in encoder-decoder translation, sharply improving quality on long sentences. The attention weights also offered an interpretable view of which inputs influenced each output. Attention proved foundational, becoming the central mechanism of the later Transformer.
events.impact: Introduced attention, the precursor to the Transformer.

events.title: Attention Is All You Need
events.short: Replaces recurrence with pure self-attention — the Transformer.
events.long: The Transformer drops recurrence and convolution entirely, letting every token attend to every other token in parallel via scaled dot-product self-attention. Multi-head attention and positional encodings let it model relationships across a whole sequence at once, while parallelism made training far more efficient than RNNs. This solved the long-range-dependency and throughput limits that had constrained sequence models. It became the architectural substrate of essentially every modern large language model.
events.impact: Became the architectural substrate of essentially every modern LLM.

events.title: BERT: Pre-training of Deep Bidirectional Transformers
events.short: Pretrains a bidirectional Transformer with masked-language modeling.
events.long: BERT pretrains a Transformer encoder using masked-language modeling and next-sentence prediction, learning context from both the left and right of each token at once. This bidirectional pretraining produced rich contextual representations that transferred broadly. Simply fine-tuning the pretrained model set new state of the art across many language-understanding benchmarks. It cemented the pretrain-then-fine-tune paradigm that came to dominate NLP.
events.impact: Established the pretrain-then-fine-tune paradigm for NLP.

events.title: Improving Language Understanding by Generative Pre-Training
events.short: Pretrains a generative Transformer decoder, then fine-tunes on tasks.
events.long: GPT pretrains a Transformer decoder on next-token prediction over large unlabeled text, then adapts it to downstream tasks with light supervised fine-tuning. It showed that a single generative pretraining objective transfers broadly across diverse language-understanding benchmarks. This decoder-only, generative approach contrasted with bidirectional encoders and proved highly scalable. It launched the GPT line that would drive much of the subsequent progress in language models.
events.impact: Launched the generative-pretraining (GPT) line of models.

events.title: Language Models are Unsupervised Multitask Learners
events.short: Shows a large LM performs many tasks zero-shot from plain prompting.
events.long: GPT-2 scales generative pretraining to 1.5 billion parameters trained on a large, diverse web corpus. It demonstrated surprising zero-shot ability on translation, summarization, and question answering with no task-specific training, simply from prompting. Its fluent, coherent text generation prompted OpenAI to adopt a staged, cautious release over misuse concerns. The work strengthened the case that scale alone produces emergent zero-shot capabilities.
events.impact: Demonstrated that scale alone yields emergent zero-shot capabilities.

events.title: Exploring the Limits of Transfer Learning with T5
events.short: Casts every NLP task as text-to-text under one unified model.
events.long: T5 reframes translation, classification, summarization, and question answering all as converting an input text string to an output text string under one model and objective. Trained on the large cleaned C4 corpus, it enabled a single architecture to handle many tasks uniformly. The paper's systematic study of architectures, objectives, datasets, and scale clarified which choices actually drive transfer-learning gains. Its text-to-text framing influenced how later models unified task formats.
events.impact: Unified diverse NLP tasks under a single text-to-text framework.

events.title: RoBERTa: A Robustly Optimized BERT Pretraining Approach
events.short: Retunes BERT pretraining — more data, longer training, no NSP.
events.long: RoBERTa shows that BERT had been significantly undertrained, and that careful changes to the recipe yield large gains. Training longer on much more data with bigger batches, dynamic masking, and dropping the next-sentence-prediction objective substantially improved results. The study isolated which design choices mattered and which did not. It demonstrated that the pretraining recipe and scale can matter as much as architectural innovation.
events.impact: Showed pretraining recipe and scale can outweigh architecture changes.

events.title: Transformer-XL: Attentive Language Models Beyond a Fixed Context
events.short: Extends Transformer context with segment recurrence and relative positions.
events.long: Transformer-XL caches and reuses hidden states computed for prior segments, introducing a form of recurrence at the segment level. Combined with relative positional encodings, this lets attention reach beyond a single fixed window without breaking positional consistency. The design captures much longer-range dependencies and avoids fragmenting context at segment boundaries. It was an influential early step toward modeling the long contexts later LLMs would emphasize.
events.impact: Pushed Transformers toward modeling much longer context.

events.title: Scaling Laws for Neural Language Models
events.short: Finds LM loss falls as a smooth power law in model, data, and compute.
events.long: The paper empirically shows that test loss decreases predictably as a power law in the number of parameters, dataset size, and training compute, holding across many orders of magnitude. These smooth relationships let practitioners forecast performance and choose how to allocate compute before committing to a large training run. The findings reframed model development as an exercise in scaling along predictable curves. They gave the subsequent push toward ever-larger models a quantitative foundation.
events.impact: Gave scaling a quantitative, predictable foundation.

events.title: Language Models are Few-Shot Learners
events.short: A 175B model performs new tasks from a few in-context examples.
events.long: GPT-3 scales generative pretraining to 175 billion parameters and shows strong few-shot, one-shot, and zero-shot performance by conditioning on examples placed directly in the prompt. This in-context learning requires no gradient updates, letting a single frozen model adapt to many tasks on the fly. The result demonstrated that scale unlocks qualitatively new capabilities. It popularized prompting as the primary way to steer large models and set the stage for the assistant era.
events.impact: Popularized in-context learning and prompting at scale.

events.title: Retrieval-Augmented Generation
events.short: Couples a generator with a retriever over an external document index.
events.long: RAG retrieves relevant passages from a non-parametric document index and conditions a sequence generator on them, training retrieval and generation together. Grounding outputs in retrieved evidence improves factual accuracy on knowledge-intensive tasks and lets the knowledge base be updated without retraining the model. This separates what the model knows from what it can look up at inference time. Retrieval grounding became a core pattern for building factual, current LLM applications.
events.impact: Established retrieval grounding as a core pattern for knowledge-intensive LLMs.

events.title: LoRA: Low-Rank Adaptation of Large Language Models
events.short: Fine-tunes LLMs by training small low-rank weight updates.
events.long: LoRA freezes the pretrained weights and learns small low-rank update matrices injected into each layer, drastically reducing the number of trainable parameters and the memory needed to adapt a model. Despite touching only a fraction of the parameters, it matches the quality of full fine-tuning on many tasks. The resulting adapters are tiny, making them cheap to store, share, and swap per task. It made customizing large models affordable and became a cornerstone of parameter-efficient fine-tuning.
events.impact: Made fine-tuning large models affordable and modular.

events.title: Evaluating Large Language Models Trained on Code
events.short: Fine-tunes GPT on code and introduces the HumanEval benchmark.
events.long: Codex fine-tunes GPT models on large amounts of public source code to generate functions from natural-language docstrings. The paper introduced the HumanEval pass@k benchmark for measuring functional correctness of generated programs. Codex powered GitHub Copilot, putting LLM code generation directly into developers' editors. It demonstrated that language models can translate intent into working programs and brought AI assistance into mainstream software development.
events.impact: Brought LLM code generation into mainstream developer tooling.

events.title: Switch Transformers: Scaling to Trillion-Parameter Models
events.short: Routes each token to one expert, scaling parameters at fixed compute.
events.long: The Switch Transformer replaces dense feed-forward layers with a sparse mixture of experts and routes each token to just a single expert, simplifying earlier multi-expert routing schemes. Because only one expert activates per token, parameter count can grow without a matching increase in per-token compute. This decoupling enabled models with up to a trillion parameters and faster training at scale. It advanced sparse mixture-of-experts as a practical route to scaling that later frontier models would adopt.
events.impact: Advanced sparse mixture-of-experts as a route to scaling.

events.title: PaLM: Scaling Language Modeling with Pathways
events.short: Trains a 540B dense Transformer showing strong reasoning gains.
events.long: PaLM is a 540-billion-parameter dense Transformer trained efficiently across thousands of accelerators using Google's Pathways system. It achieved breakthrough results on reasoning, multilingual, and code benchmarks, with especially strong gains when paired with chain-of-thought prompting. The model offered evidence that certain capabilities emerge sharply as scale increases. It demonstrated the reasoning potential at the frontier of dense-model scale before sparse and reasoning-trained models took over.
events.impact: Demonstrated emergent reasoning at the frontier of dense-model scale.

events.title: Training Compute-Optimal Large Language Models
events.short: Shows models were undertrained — scale data and parameters together.
events.long: Chinchilla revisits scaling laws and finds that for a fixed compute budget, prior frontier models were too large and trained on too few tokens. A 70-billion-parameter model trained on far more data outperformed several much larger models. The result implied that parameters and training data should be scaled together rather than just inflating model size. It reshaped how labs allocate compute and made data quantity a central lever in model design.
events.impact: Redefined compute-optimal scaling toward more training data.

events.title: FlashAttention: Fast and Memory-Efficient Exact Attention
events.short: Computes exact attention with an IO-aware, memory-efficient GPU kernel.
events.long: FlashAttention reorders the attention computation to tile and fuse operations within fast on-chip GPU memory, avoiding ever materializing the full attention matrix in slower memory. This IO-aware design yields mathematically exact results while sharply cutting memory use and wall-clock time. The savings make training faster and allow models to handle substantially longer sequences. It became a standard kernel adopted across the ecosystem and a key enabler of long-context models.
events.impact: Became a standard kernel that sped up training and extended context.

events.title: Training Language Models to Follow Instructions
events.short: Aligns an LLM to instructions using reinforcement learning from human feedback.
events.long: InstructGPT first fine-tunes GPT-3 on human-written demonstrations, then trains a reward model from human preference rankings and optimizes the policy against it with reinforcement learning. The resulting model follows instructions far more reliably and is preferred by human raters even over a much larger base model. It also reduced harmful and untruthful outputs relative to the base model. This three-stage RLHF recipe became the standard approach to aligning assistant models, including ChatGPT.
events.impact: Made RLHF the standard recipe for aligning assistant models.

events.title: ChatGPT
events.short: Launches a conversational, RLHF-tuned assistant to the public.
events.long: ChatGPT packaged an instruction-tuned, RLHF-aligned GPT model into a free, easy-to-use conversational interface released in November 2022. Its accessibility and surprising versatility drove some of the fastest user adoption ever seen for a software product. It brought large language models out of research and into everyday public use. The launch catalyzed the generative-AI boom and an industry-wide race to ship competing assistants.
events.impact: Brought LLMs to a mass audience and catalyzed the generative-AI boom.

events.title: GPT-4 Technical Report
events.short: A large multimodal model with strong reasoning and exam-level performance.
events.long: GPT-4 accepts both image and text input and produces text, scoring at human-professional levels on many academic and professional exams. The report emphasizes capability, predictable scaling, and extensive safety and alignment evaluation rather than disclosing architectural or training details. It showed markedly improved reasoning and reliability over its predecessors across diverse benchmarks. It set a new capability bar that competitors spent the following years chasing.
events.impact: Set a new capability bar for frontier multimodal LLMs.

events.title: LLaMA: Open and Efficient Foundation Language Models
events.short: Releases efficient foundation models trained only on public data.
events.long: LLaMA is a family of 7B–65B foundation models trained solely on publicly available datasets, with smaller models matching far larger ones by training on more tokens. Released to the research community, its weights quickly spread and seeded a wave of open experimentation, fine-tunes, and tooling. It showed that strong models could be built without proprietary data and run on modest hardware. The release effectively catalyzed the modern open-weight LLM ecosystem.
events.impact: Catalyzed the open-weight LLM ecosystem.

events.title: Llama 2: Open Foundation and Fine-Tuned Chat Models
events.short: Open-license base and chat models with documented RLHF tuning.
events.long: Llama 2 improves on the original with more training data and longer context, and adds chat models aligned through supervised fine-tuning and RLHF. Meta documented the alignment and safety process in detail and released the models under a license permitting most commercial use. This made genuinely capable open models available for products, not just research. It substantially broadened access and accelerated commercial adoption of open-weight LLMs.
events.impact: Made commercially usable open chat models widely available.

events.title: Mistral 7B
events.short: A compact 7B model that outperforms larger open models of its time.
events.long: Mistral 7B uses grouped-query attention and sliding-window attention to deliver fast, memory-efficient inference over longer sequences. Despite its small size, it outperformed larger contemporaneous open models on many benchmarks. Released openly under a permissive license, it became a popular base for fine-tuning and deployment. It showed that careful architecture and training can let small, efficient open models punch well above their size.
events.impact: Showed small, efficient open models can punch above their size.

events.title: Direct Preference Optimization
events.short: Aligns models from preference data without a separate reward model or RL.
events.long: DPO reparameterizes the RLHF objective so that human preferences can be optimized directly on the policy with a simple classification-style loss. This removes the need to train a separate reward model and to run reinforcement learning, which were the most complex and unstable parts of the alignment pipeline. The approach is simpler to implement and tends to train more stably. It became a widely used, RL-free alternative for aligning models to human preferences.
events.impact: Offered a simpler, RL-free alternative to RLHF for alignment.

events.title: Gemini: A Family of Highly Capable Multimodal Models
events.short: Natively multimodal models spanning text, image, audio, and video.
events.long: Gemini is built to be multimodal from the ground up, processing and reasoning jointly across text, images, audio, and video within a single model. The family spans sizes from compact on-device variants to a frontier model, reporting strong results across multimodal and reasoning benchmarks. Its native multimodality contrasted with approaches that bolt vision onto a text model after the fact. It marked Google DeepMind's unified push to the multimodal frontier.
events.impact: Advanced natively multimodal modeling at the frontier.

events.title: Claude 3 Model Family
events.short: A tiered model family (Haiku, Sonnet, Opus) with vision and stronger reasoning.
events.long: Claude 3 introduced three models — Haiku, Sonnet, and Opus — that trade off speed, cost, and capability across the lineup. The family added image understanding and improved reasoning, coding, and multilingual performance over prior Claude models. It targeted enterprise use with longer context windows and fewer unnecessary refusals. The release strengthened Anthropic's standing as a serious frontier competitor.
events.impact: Strengthened Anthropic's competitive frontier model lineup.

events.title: GPT-4o
events.short: An omni model handling text, audio, and vision with low-latency responses.
events.long: GPT-4o ('omni') is a single model trained jointly across text, audio, and image inputs and outputs, enabling near-real-time spoken conversation. It matched GPT-4-level text quality while being faster and cheaper to serve, with notably stronger audio and vision. Handling speech end to end within one model reduced latency compared with chaining separate components. It brought fluid, real-time multimodal interaction to a mainstream consumer assistant.
events.impact: Brought real-time multimodal interaction to a mainstream assistant.

events.title: o1: Learning to Reason with LLMs
events.short: Trains a model to reason via long internal chains at inference time.
events.long: o1 is trained with reinforcement learning to produce a long internal chain of thought before giving its final answer, spending more inference-time compute on harder problems. This sharply improved performance on math, science, and coding tasks that require careful multi-step reasoning. It introduced test-time compute as a new axis of scaling distinct from growing the model or its training data. The approach reframed how frontier capability could be pushed and spawned a wave of reasoning models.
events.impact: Popularized inference-time reasoning as a new scaling axis.

events.title: DeepSeek-R1
events.short: Open-weight reasoning model trained largely via pure RL, rivaling OpenAI o1 at a fraction of the cost.
events.long: Released January 2025 with an accompanying paper (arXiv:2501.12948), DeepSeek-R1 showed that large-scale reinforcement learning alone could induce sophisticated chain-of-thought reasoning without supervised fine-tuning on human reasoning traces. The full model adds a cold-start fine-tuning stage and distills its reasoning into smaller dense models. It was released openly under an MIT license and reportedly matched OpenAI's o1 on math and coding benchmarks at far lower training cost. Its release triggered a major market reaction and accelerated the global open reasoning-model race.
events.impact: Proved pure-RL reasoning works and made frontier-grade reasoning openly available.

events.title: OpenAI o3-mini
events.short: Cost-efficient reasoning model and OpenAI's first o3-series release, free to all ChatGPT users.
events.long: Released January 2025, o3-mini was the first generally available model from OpenAI's o3 reasoning family, offering selectable reasoning effort (low/medium/high) and strong STEM and coding performance at much lower cost than o1. It was the first reasoning model OpenAI made available to free-tier ChatGPT users. The full o3 and o4-mini models followed in April 2025. It helped cement test-time compute reasoning models as a mainstream product tier early in 2025.
events.impact: Made frontier reasoning cheap and broadly accessible, including a free tier.

events.title: Grok 3
events.short: xAI's flagship trained on a ~200k-GPU cluster, adding Think and Big Brain reasoning modes.
events.long: Released February 2025, Grok 3 was trained with roughly ten times the compute of its predecessor using xAI's Colossus supercomputer. It introduced reasoning via a Think mode and a compute-heavy Big Brain mode, alongside a faster Grok 3 mini variant. xAI reported competitive results against leading models on math and reasoning benchmarks. It showed how quickly a young lab could reach the frontier given massive concentrated compute.
events.impact: Showed a young lab could reach the frontier through sheer compute scale.

events.title: Claude 3.7 Sonnet
events.short: First hybrid reasoning model offering near-instant or extended visible thinking in one model.
events.long: Released February 2025, Claude 3.7 Sonnet was the first model to combine quick responses and extended step-by-step reasoning in a single model, with a user-controllable thinking budget. It delivered notable gains in coding and front-end web development and led agentic-coding benchmarks at launch. Anthropic shipped it alongside Claude Code, its agentic command-line coding tool. It established the one-model-two-modes hybrid-reasoning pattern that competitors quickly adopted.
events.impact: Popularized hybrid reasoning and anchored Anthropic's coding-agent strategy.

events.title: GPT-4.5
events.short: OpenAI's largest non-reasoning model, scaling pretraining for knowledge, EQ, and fewer hallucinations.
events.long: Released February 2025, GPT-4.5 was OpenAI's biggest scaled-up pretrained chat model, focused on broader knowledge, stronger instruction-following, higher emotional intelligence, and reduced hallucination rather than chain-of-thought reasoning. OpenAI described it as a very large, expensive model and framed it as its last non-chain-of-thought release. It signaled a strategic pivot from pure pretraining scaling toward reasoning-centric models. It marked a visible inflection point where pretraining scaling showed diminishing returns versus reasoning approaches.
events.impact: Marked the end of OpenAI's pure-pretraining-scaling era before the reasoning pivot.

events.title: Gemini 2.5 Pro
events.short: Google's thinking flagship with built-in reasoning and a 1M-token (expanding to 2M) context window.
events.long: Announced March 2025, Gemini 2.5 Pro made reasoning a default behavior, thinking through its response before answering, and topped many reasoning, coding, and multimodal leaderboards at launch. It shipped with a one-million-token context window, with a planned expansion to two million, and was offered free in Google AI Studio. It quickly became one of the most-used frontier models of 2025, especially for long-context and coding work. It reasserted Google as a top-tier frontier competitor.
events.impact: Made Google's flagship a leading reasoning and long-context model.

events.title: Llama 4 (Scout & Maverick)
events.short: Meta's first natively multimodal, mixture-of-experts open-weight models, with very long context.
events.long: Released April 2025, the Llama 4 herd debuted Scout and Maverick, Meta's first mixture-of-experts and natively multimodal models using early fusion of text and vision. Scout was designed to fit on a single high-end GPU and advertised an industry-leading multi-million-token context window, while Maverick claimed wins over leading competitors on several benchmarks. Meta also previewed a still-training Behemoth model as a teacher. The release drew scrutiny over benchmark presentation and marked a rockier moment for Meta's open-weight leadership.
events.impact: Brought mixture-of-experts and native multimodality to Meta's open-weight line.

events.title: Qwen3
events.short: Alibaba's open-weight hybrid-reasoning family spanning small dense to large MoE models.
events.long: Released April 2025, Qwen3 was Alibaba's first hybrid-reasoning family, switching between a thinking mode for complex tasks and a fast non-thinking mode. It spans dense models from under a billion to tens of billions of parameters plus large mixture-of-experts variants, all released open-weight. Its flagship posted results competitive with other leading reasoning models. It became one of the most widely adopted open-weight model families of 2025.
events.impact: Strengthened open-weight frontier competition across a full model-size range.

events.title: Claude Opus 4 & Sonnet 4
events.short: Anthropic's Claude 4 family, hybrid models built for long-horizon agentic coding and tool use.
events.long: Announced May 2025, the Claude 4 family (Opus 4 and Sonnet 4) are hybrid models offering instant answers or extended thinking, with parallel tool use, tool calls during reasoning, and local-file memory. Opus 4 was positioned as a state-of-the-art coding and agentic model capable of sustained multi-hour autonomous work. Claude Code reached general availability the same day, making agentic coding the family's headline use case. It anchored Anthropic's strong position in AI-assisted software development through 2025.
events.impact: Set the bar for long-horizon agentic coding and powered Claude Code's surge.

events.title: GPT-5
events.short: OpenAI's unified flagship that automatically routes between fast answers and deeper reasoning.
events.long: Released August 2025, GPT-5 is a unified system with a router that decides when to answer quickly versus when to think longer, folding the separate o-series reasoning track into the main model line. OpenAI reported state-of-the-art results across coding, math, writing, health, and multimodal perception, with reduced hallucination. It rolled out across ChatGPT and the API simultaneously and became the default model for most users. It marked the convergence of OpenAI's reasoning and chat lineages into a single product.
events.impact: Unified reasoning and chat into one auto-routing flagship for all users.

events.title: gpt-oss-120b & gpt-oss-20b
events.short: OpenAI's first open-weight models since GPT-2, Apache-2.0 reasoning models that run on local hardware.
events.long: Released August 2025 under an Apache 2.0 license, gpt-oss-120b and gpt-oss-20b were OpenAI's first open-weight language models in years, designed for agentic workflows with tool use, web search, and code execution and selectable reasoning effort. The larger model was designed to run on a single high-end GPU while the smaller targets edge devices. The release reversed OpenAI's long-standing closed-weights stance and was distributed across major model hubs. It significantly raised the bar for openly available reasoning models.
events.impact: Reversed OpenAI's closed stance with strong locally-runnable open-weight reasoning models.

events.title: Chain-of-Thought Prompting Elicits Reasoning
events.short: Prompting for intermediate steps unlocks multi-step reasoning in LLMs.
events.long: The paper shows that prompting a model to produce a series of intermediate reasoning steps before its final answer substantially improves performance on arithmetic, commonsense, and symbolic tasks. The technique requires no fine-tuning, working purely through how the prompt is phrased. The benefit emerges mainly in sufficiently large models, an example of capabilities appearing with scale. Chain-of-thought became a core prompting technique and the conceptual seed for later reasoning-trained models.
events.impact: Established step-by-step prompting as a core reasoning technique.

events.title: ReAct: Synergizing Reasoning and Acting
events.short: Interleaves chain-of-thought reasoning with tool/environment actions.
events.long: ReAct prompts a model to alternate 'thought' steps with 'action' steps that call tools or query an environment, feeding the resulting observations back into the next thought. Interleaving reasoning and acting grounds the model's plans in external state rather than its own assumptions. This reduces hallucination on knowledge-intensive and decision-making tasks and makes the agent's behavior more interpretable. The reason-act-observe loop it defined sits at the core of most modern LLM agents.
events.impact: Defined the reason-act-observe loop at the core of modern LLM agents.

events.title: Toolformer: Language Models Can Teach Themselves to Use Tools
events.short: A model self-supervises when and how to call external APIs.
events.long: Toolformer inserts candidate API calls into its own training text, then keeps only those calls that actually reduce prediction loss and fine-tunes on the filtered result. Through this self-supervised process the model learns which tool to invoke, when, and with what arguments, without any hand-labeled tool-use data. The trained model can call calculators, search, and other APIs to improve its answers. It showed that tool-use behavior can be learned automatically rather than hand-engineered.
events.impact: Showed models can learn tool use in a self-supervised way.

events.title: HuggingGPT: Solving Tasks with ChatGPT and its Friends
events.short: Uses an LLM as a controller that orchestrates specialized models.
events.long: HuggingGPT uses an LLM as a controller that plans a task, selects appropriate expert models from a hub, dispatches subtasks to them, and assembles their outputs into a final result. This lets a language model coordinate specialized vision, speech, and other models to solve complex multimodal requests it could not handle alone. The LLM effectively acts as a planner and router over a toolbox of models. It demonstrated the pattern of an LLM orchestrating many specialist models.
events.impact: Demonstrated an LLM as an orchestrator of many specialist models.

events.title: Reflexion: Language Agents with Verbal Reinforcement Learning
events.short: Agents improve by reflecting in words on past failures across trials.
events.long: Reflexion has an agent generate verbal self-critiques of its failed attempts and store them in memory to guide subsequent tries, all without updating any model weights. This linguistic feedback acts like a lightweight form of reinforcement learning expressed in natural language. Across repeated trials, the accumulated reflections steadily improve the agent's success rate. It improved performance on decision-making, reasoning, and coding tasks using only prompting and memory.
events.impact: Introduced verbal self-reflection as a lightweight agent-improvement loop.

events.title: Generative Agents: Interactive Simulacra of Human Behavior
events.short: LLM agents with memory and reflection simulate believable daily lives.
events.long: The work places LLM-driven agents in a sandbox town, giving each a memory stream that it retrieves from, reflects on to form higher-level insights, and plans its day from. The agents formed relationships and produced emergent social behavior, such as autonomously organizing and attending a party, in believable ways. The architecture of memory, reflection, and planning proved central to this believability. It showed how relatively simple cognitive structures can make autonomous agents feel lifelike.
events.impact: Showed memory and reflection enable believable autonomous agents.

events.title: Tree of Thoughts: Deliberate Problem Solving with LLMs
events.short: Explores and evaluates multiple reasoning branches via deliberate search.
events.long: Tree of Thoughts generalizes chain-of-thought by maintaining a tree of partial solutions, letting the model generate, evaluate, and compare multiple reasoning branches. With lookahead and backtracking, it can search deliberately rather than committing to a single line of reasoning. This structured exploration markedly improved performance on tasks requiring planning or search, such as puzzles. It brought classical search strategies to bear on LLM problem solving.
events.impact: Brought structured search over reasoning paths to LLM problem solving.

events.title: Voyager: An Open-Ended Embodied Agent with LLMs
events.short: An LLM agent autonomously explores Minecraft and grows a skill library.
events.long: Voyager uses an LLM to propose its own goals, write executable code to act as reusable skills, and store those skills in a growing library it can call on later. An automatic curriculum drives it to pursue progressively harder objectives without human intervention. Through this loop it explored and acquired a widening set of capabilities open-endedly in Minecraft. It demonstrated self-directed, lifelong skill acquisition by an LLM agent in a complex environment.
events.impact: Demonstrated open-ended, self-directed skill acquisition by an LLM agent.

events.title: AutoGPT
events.short: An open-source loop that chains GPT-4 calls toward an autonomous goal.
events.long: AutoGPT wraps an LLM in a loop that decomposes a high-level goal into subtasks, executes tools and web actions, and feeds results back to plan the next step. The viral open-source project let anyone point a model at an objective and watch it attempt to pursue it autonomously. Though often brittle in practice, it captured the public imagination about self-directed AI. It popularized the idea of autonomous agent loops far beyond research circles.
events.impact: Brought autonomous agent loops to widespread public attention.

events.title: SWE-bench: Can Language Models Resolve Real-World GitHub Issues?
events.short: Benchmarks agents on fixing real issues in real codebases.
events.long: SWE-bench tasks systems with resolving genuine GitHub issues drawn from popular Python repositories, with each fix verified by the project's own test suite. Unlike toy coding benchmarks, it requires navigating large existing codebases and producing changes that actually pass tests. Early results exposed how difficult real-world software engineering is for LLMs. It became a key benchmark for measuring progress on coding agents and a target the field has steadily climbed.
events.impact: Set a realistic standard for evaluating coding agents.

events.title: Devin
events.short: An autonomous software-engineer agent with its own shell, editor, and browser.
events.long: Devin is presented as an AI software engineer that plans and executes multi-step coding tasks end to end inside a sandboxed environment with its own terminal, code editor, and browser. It was demonstrated resolving issues, learning unfamiliar tools, and completing real engineering jobs with limited human input. The launch generated intense interest and debate about how autonomous coding agents really are. It pushed the vision of a fully autonomous software-engineering agent into the spotlight.
events.impact: Pushed the autonomous software-engineering agent into the spotlight.

events.title: Claude Computer Use
events.short: Lets a model operate a computer by viewing the screen and controlling input.
events.long: Computer use gives Claude the ability to perceive screenshots and control a desktop by moving the cursor, clicking, and typing to complete tasks across arbitrary applications. Released as a beta capability, it let a model operate general graphical interfaces rather than only calling predefined tools. This extended agents toward the broad range of software a human can drive. It was an early step toward general GUI-operating agents from a frontier lab.
events.impact: Enabled general GUI-driven computer operation by an LLM agent.

events.title: AutoGen: Multi-Agent Conversation Framework
events.short: A framework where multiple configurable agents converse to solve tasks.
events.long: AutoGen lets developers compose applications from multiple agents — LLM-backed, tool-using, or human-in-the-loop — that collaborate through structured conversation. Its flexible orchestration supports patterns ranging from pair-programming to group problem solving and human oversight. Developers can configure each agent's role, tools, and behavior and let them negotiate solutions among themselves. It provided a reusable, widely adopted framework for building multi-agent systems.
events.impact: Provided a reusable framework for building multi-agent systems.

events.title: Model Context Protocol
events.short: An open standard for connecting LLMs to tools and data sources.
events.long: MCP defines a standard client-server protocol for exposing tools, resources, and prompts to language models, so any compliant model can connect to any compliant integration. It aims to replace bespoke, one-off connectors with a shared, interoperable interface for the growing agent ecosystem. Introduced by Anthropic in late 2024, it gained rapid traction, and in 2025 OpenAI, Google, and Microsoft adopted MCP, turning it into a cross-vendor industry standard. That broad adoption made it the de facto way agents connect to external tools and data.
events.impact: Standardized how agents connect to external tools and data.

events.title: OpenAI Operator & Computer-Using Agent
events.short: Browser-driving agent that clicks, types, and navigates websites via a vision-RL model.
events.long: Launched January 2025 as a research preview, Operator was powered by the Computer-Using Agent model, which combines vision with reinforcement-learning-trained reasoning to operate graphical interfaces the way a person does. It clicked buttons, filled forms, and scrolled to complete web tasks, posting the strongest computer-use benchmark numbers shipped to date at launch. It was among the first major consumer-facing autonomous web agents from a frontier lab. It helped kick off 2025's wave of agent products.
events.impact: Brought autonomous computer-use agents from research demos into a shipped product.

events.title: OpenAI Responses API & Agents SDK
events.short: New agent-building stack: a unified Responses API plus an open-source multi-agent orchestration SDK.
events.long: Announced March 2025, OpenAI shipped the Responses API, a new primitive merging chat completions and assistants with built-in web search, file search, and computer-use tools, alongside the open-source Agents SDK. The Agents SDK is a production-grade successor to Swarm for orchestrating multi-agent workflows, handoffs, and tracing, and can even drive non-OpenAI models. Together they let developers build their own Operator- and deep-research-style agents. The stack became foundational infrastructure for the 2025 agent-developer ecosystem.
events.impact: Gave developers a standard production stack for building autonomous agents.

events.title: Agent2Agent (A2A) Protocol
events.short: Open protocol letting AI agents from different vendors discover each other and delegate tasks.
events.long: Announced April 2025 at Google Cloud Next with dozens of partners, A2A is an open specification for how autonomous agents from different vendors and frameworks discover one another, exchange information securely, and coordinate work. It complements MCP, which connects agents to tools, by standardizing agent-to-agent communication. A2A was later open-sourced under Apache 2.0 and donated to the Linux Foundation. It became the leading candidate for cross-vendor multi-agent interoperability.
events.impact: Established an open standard for cross-vendor agent-to-agent collaboration.

events.title: Claude Code (general availability)
events.short: Agentic terminal coding tool that delegates real software tasks to an autonomous agent.
events.long: First previewed in February 2025 and released broadly in May 2025, Claude Code is an agentic command-line tool that lets developers delegate coding tasks in natural language, with background task support and native editor integrations. It rapidly reached a very large run-rate revenue, among the fastest products ever to do so, with adoption across major technology companies. It became emblematic of 2025 as the year agentic coding went mainstream. Its success reshaped developer workflows, often moving work out of the traditional IDE.
events.impact: Made agentic terminal-based coding mainstream and a billion-dollar product category.

brief.title: Foundations of AI
brief.short: The field is named and given its first concrete goals and learning machines.
brief.long: In under a decade AI moved from Turing's thought experiment about machine intelligence to the Dartmouth workshop that named the field, to Rosenblatt's perceptron — a physical machine that learned from examples. Together they set the questions, the discipline, and the first learning model.
brief.impact: Established AI's founding question, its name, and the first trainable neuron.

brief.title: Neural Networks Mature
brief.short: From a near-fatal critique to backprop, CNNs, and LSTMs that actually work.
brief.long: After Minsky and Papert exposed the limits of single-layer networks, backpropagation made multi-layer training feasible, convolutional nets brought it to images, and LSTMs solved long-range dependencies in sequences. The period rebuilt neural networks into practical, trainable architectures.
brief.impact: Forged the core trainable architectures underpinning modern deep learning.

brief.title: The Deep-Learning Toolkit
brief.short: Generative models and training tricks that made deep nets reliable to train.
brief.long: A burst of practical advances followed AlexNet: GANs opened adversarial generative modeling, while Adam, residual connections, and batch normalization made deep networks faster, deeper, and far more stable to train. These tools became default ingredients of the field.
brief.impact: Supplied the optimizer, normalization, and depth tricks now standard everywhere.

brief.title: Embeddings and Attention
brief.short: Words become dense vectors and decoders learn to attend to source context.
brief.long: Word2vec turned words into dense vectors that captured meaning and analogy, while attention let translation models focus on the most relevant source words instead of one fixed summary. Together they laid the representational and mechanistic groundwork for the Transformer.
brief.impact: Set up the embedding and attention ideas the Transformer would unify.

brief.title: The Pretraining Explosion
brief.short: Transformers are pretrained at scale, splitting into encoder and decoder lines.
brief.long: BERT and RoBERTa showed bidirectional encoder pretraining, GPT-1 and GPT-2 advanced generative decoders, T5 unified tasks as text-to-text, and Transformer-XL extended context. Within two years pretraining became the dominant paradigm across NLP.
brief.impact: Made large-scale pretraining the default approach to language tasks.

brief.title: Scaling and Grounding
brief.short: Scaling laws, a 175B few-shot model, and retrieval grounding arrive together.
brief.long: Scaling laws made performance gains predictable, GPT-3 turned that scale into few-shot in-context learning, and RAG showed how to ground generation in retrieved knowledge. The year established both the promise of scale and a way to anchor it in facts.
brief.impact: Quantified scaling and demonstrated in-context learning and retrieval grounding.

brief.title: Efficient and Aligned
brief.short: Models get cheaper to adapt, faster to run, and tuned to follow instructions.
brief.long: LoRA and sparse mixture-of-experts cut adaptation and compute costs, FlashAttention and Chinchilla's data-optimal scaling improved efficiency, Codex and PaLM pushed capability, and InstructGPT's RLHF made models follow human instructions. Frontier models became both more efficient and more usable.
brief.impact: Combined efficiency advances with RLHF alignment into the modern LLM recipe.

brief.title: Frontier and Open Models
brief.short: Multimodal frontier models race ahead while open weights proliferate.
brief.long: GPT-4, Gemini, Claude 3, and GPT-4o pushed multimodal frontier capability while o1 added inference-time reasoning; in parallel LLaMA, Llama 2, and Mistral built an open-weight ecosystem and DPO simplified alignment. Closed frontier and open communities advanced together.
brief.impact: Cemented multimodal frontier models alongside a thriving open-weight ecosystem.

brief.title: Reasoning Goes Mainstream
brief.short: Open and closed labs alike ship 'thinking' models as the new default.
brief.long: In the first months of 2025 reasoning models went from novelty to norm: DeepSeek-R1 proved open, pure-RL chain-of-thought reasoning was possible and matched o1 cheaply, while o3-mini made reasoning cheap enough for free ChatGPT users. Anthropic's Claude 3.7 Sonnet and Google's Gemini 2.5 Pro folded extended thinking into single hybrid models, and GPT-4.5 arrived as OpenAI's last pretraining-first release. Together these launches established test-time reasoning as the default frontier paradigm across both open and closed labs. The competition spanned vendors and continents within a single quarter.
brief.impact: Made test-time reasoning the default paradigm across open and closed frontier models.

brief.title: Open Weights & GPT-5
brief.short: A strong open-weight wave meets unified frontier flagships built for agentic coding.
brief.long: Mid-2025 saw a powerful open-weight surge — Meta's mixture-of-experts Llama 4, Alibaba's hybrid-reasoning Qwen3, and even OpenAI's own Apache-licensed gpt-oss — narrowing the gap with closed labs. At the frontier, Anthropic's Claude 4 and OpenAI's GPT-5 unified fast answers and extended reasoning into single systems, with GPT-5 auto-routing between them. Agentic coding emerged as the headline capability these flagships were built to deliver. The result was a market where open weights and unified reasoning flagships advanced in parallel.
brief.impact: Paired a strong open-weight wave with unified reasoning-and-chat frontier flagships.

brief.title: Reasoning and Acting
brief.short: Prompting for explicit reasoning, then interleaving it with actions.
brief.long: Chain-of-thought showed that eliciting intermediate reasoning steps unlocks multi-step problem solving, and ReAct combined that reasoning with tool and environment actions in a loop. Together they turned a passive text model into something that can think and act.
brief.impact: Defined the reason-and-act foundation of LLM agents.

brief.title: Autonomous Agents
brief.short: Agents learn tools, self-reflect, search, remember, and run open-ended loops.
brief.long: A rapid wave gave agents new abilities: self-taught tool use (Toolformer), model orchestration (HuggingGPT), verbal self-reflection (Reflexion), memory-driven simulacra (Generative Agents), deliberate search (Tree of Thoughts), open-ended skill learning (Voyager), and viral autonomous loops (AutoGPT). The agent paradigm took shape.
brief.impact: Assembled tool use, memory, reflection, and search into autonomous agents.

brief.title: Agents at Work
brief.short: Agents take on real software engineering, computer use, and team collaboration.
brief.long: SWE-bench measured agents on real GitHub issues, Devin showcased an end-to-end autonomous software engineer, Claude's computer use enabled general GUI control, and AutoGen framed multi-agent collaboration. Agents moved from demos toward genuine work.
brief.impact: Moved agents toward real-world engineering and computer-operation tasks.

brief.title: The Agent Stack
brief.short: Agents become shipped products, protocols, and breakout coding tools.
brief.long: In 2025 agents turned from research demos into shipped products and standards. OpenAI's Operator put an autonomous computer-using browser agent in front of consumers, while its Responses API and Agents SDK gave developers a production stack for building their own. Google's Agent2Agent protocol standardized how agents from different vendors discover and delegate to one another, complementing MCP's tool connections. Anthropic's generally available Claude Code became the breakout agentic coding tool, marking the year agents went to work in earnest.
brief.impact: Turned agents into shipped products, interoperability standards, and breakout coding tools.
